critic_raw:
1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON LONGITUDINAL PATIENT DATA IS METHODOLOGICALLY INCOHERENT: The central claim is that 'each patient's timeline is independent after accounting for observed variables.' This is both unverifiable and implausible for a chronic pain cohort followed over 3 years. Patients within a clinic share treatment protocols, referring physicians, and temporal policy changes. A random split means a patient's early visits could be in training and later visits (which reveal outcome) could be in validation — this is temporal leakage within patient. More fundamentally, the RNN is designed to exploit sequence order, but if sequences are broken across splits, the model cannot learn what it is designed to learn. Evidence: demonstrate that no patient has records in both train and test; demonstrate that test sequences begin after all training sequences for any given patient.

2. COMPARISON BASELINE IS STRUCTURALLY WEAKER BY DESIGN: The RNN operates on temporal symptom sequences; the logistic regression uses only 'demographic and static clinical features.' These are not the same input space. The experiment does not test 'RNN vs. logistic regression' — it tests 'temporal sequences vs. static features.' A logistic regression trained on the same temporal features (lagged variables, aggregated symptom counts, baseline pain scores) would be a closer baseline. The current design conflates input representation choice with model architecture choice, making it impossible to attribute any performance gap to the RNN's sequence modeling rather than the richer feature set. Evidence: add a logistic regression trained on engineered temporal aggregates and compare its performance to the RNN.

3. MAE ON QUALITY-OF-LIFE SCORES PRESUPPOSES INTERVAL SCALE VALIDITY: The design uses MAE as primary metric for quality-of-life improvement scores. This is appropriate only if the QoL scale is interval-scaled and errors are symmetric around zero. Most validated QoL instruments (e.g., SF-36, PROMIS) have ceiling effects and floor effects — improvement from 80 to 90 is clinically different from improvement from 20 to 30. An MAE of 5 points means different things at different parts of the scale. No mention is made of whether a clinically meaningful difference threshold was used to bound acceptable MAE. Evidence: report error distributions stratified by baseline score decile; a heteroskedastic error distribution would confirm scale invalidity.

4. 1,500 PATIENTS IS LIKELY UNDERPOWERED FOR AN RNN WITH ATTENTION: RNNs with attention require enough patients to learn attention weights that generalize. With 1,500 patients, a 70/15/15 split gives ~225 test patients. For a regression task on continuous QoL scores, this may have adequate power for a single comparison, but the design includes 'tokenization of unstructured symptom notes,' which substantially expands the input dimensionality. No sample size justification is provided. The risk is that the model overfits to symptom note vocabulary present in training but not test. Evidence: plot validation loss curves; if they do not converge before test evaluation, the model is underpowered.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on longitudinal patient data breaks patient sequences and enables temporal leakage
Baseline uses weaker input representation (static features only), conflating feature choice with architecture choice
MAE on quality-of-life scores assumes interval scale validity that most QoL instruments do not satisfy
Sample size of 1,500 patients likely underpowered for RNN with attention over tokenized unstructured notes
Stratified random split on longitudinal records allows future observations of the same patient into the training set, constituting temporal leakage
Logistic regression baseline uses fewer features than the RNN, making the comparison a feature-set difference rather than an architecture comparison
Quality of life score as a continuous regression target ignores ceiling/floor effects, ordinal structure, and informative censoring from patient dropout
1,500-patient dataset is likely underpowered for training a multi-layer RNN with attention mechanisms
Unstructured symptom note tokenization is underspecified and risks vocabulary leakage or prospective language in clinical notes
Stratified random split on longitudinal patient data likely creates patient-level data leakage if split is at record level
Baseline uses only static features while RNN uses temporal features — comparison conflates model class with feature class
Task typed as regression but baseline is logistic regression — fundamental outcome-model mismatch
Assumption of weak temporal autocorrelation is unverified by any preliminary analysis
Treatment adherence confounder is acknowledged but not operationally controlled in model comparisons
